text:
The purpose of this study was to investigate the types of coping strategies used by families of children with chronic diseases in order to cope with the psychological and social stress arising from the care of children with chronic diseases. A quantitative cross-sectional research method was employed, and 162 parents (mostly mothers) of children suffering from chronic diseases like diabetes mellitus, bronchial asthma, juvenile rheumatoid arthritis, atopic eczema, celiac disease, epilepsy and thyroid disorders were selected as the research sample. The tool used in this study was Family Crisis Oriented Personal Evaluation Scales (F-COPES).

In this study, 59% of the parents that participated to the survey reported to have high family stress associated with their child's illness. Parental reports of burden varied strongly between diagnoses and the three diagnoses associated with the highest burden were celiac disease, juvenile rheumatoid arthritis and diabetes. In addition, the parents who took part to the study described their coping strategies towards their children's illnesses; most often they used either the reframing strategy (making stressful events appear more manageable) or the passive evaluation (moderating their reactions to stress).

Parents’ coping with their children’s chronic illness The study explored factors related to parents’ coping with their children’s chronic health conditions, with the purpose of developing a model of factors influencing the coping effectiveness. Correlates included parents’ age, gender, children’s gender, number of siblings and family structure, and parents’ socio-economic status. Analysis indicated that none of these factors were related to parents’ coping effectiveness. A more adequate use of resources in effective copers, such as parents in complete families and parents with higher education was observed. On the basis of these findings, the authors suggest that the healthcare system, and in particular pediatric nursing, should direct its intervention programmes to those high-risk families such as families with low education level and families with children with high-burden chronic disease such as celiac disease and diabetes.
